revision.text:
'''Honmatsu System''' (本末制度) was an administrative system established by the [[Edo period|Edo]] [[bakufu]] (shogunate) to regulate and control Buddhist organizations.
== Background == The ''honmatsu'' system organized Buddhist temples into a hierarchical structure, where ''honji'' (main temples) or ''honzan'' (head temples) held authority over ''matsuji'' (subordinate temples). While this system already existed during the [[Heian period]], it initially functioned as a protective relationship in which main temples supported their affiliated temples.
In the Edo period, the [[Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape.
== See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.

'''Honmatsu System''' (本末制度) was an administrative system established by the [[Edo period|Edo]] [[bakufu]] (shogunate) to regulate and control Buddhist organizations.
== Background == The ''honmatsu'' system organized Buddhist temples into a hierarchical structure, where ''honji'' (main temples) or ''honzan'' (head temples) held authority over ''matsuji'' (subordinate temples). While this system already existed during the [[Heian period]], it initially functioned as a protective relationship in which main temples supported their affiliated temples.
In the Edo period, the [[Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape.
== See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.

revision.text:
'''Honmatsu System''' (本末制度) was an administrative system established by the [[Edo period|Edo]] [[bakufu]] (shogunate) to regulate and control Buddhist organizations.
== Background == The ''honmatsu'' system organized Buddhist temples into a hierarchical structure, where ''honji'' (main temples) or ''honzan'' (head temples) held authority over ''matsuji'' (subordinate temples). While this system already existed during the [[Heian period]], it initially functioned as a protective relationship in which main temples supported their affiliated temples.
In the Edo period, the [[Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape.== See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.

'''Honmatsu System''' (本末制度) was an administrative system established by the [[Edo period|Edo]] [[bakufu]] (shogunate) to regulate and control Buddhist organizations.
== Background == The ''honmatsu'' system organized Buddhist temples into a hierarchical structure, where ''honji'' (main temples) or ''honzan'' (head temples) held authority over ''matsuji'' (subordinate temples). While this system already existed during the [[Heian period]], it initially functioned as a protective relationship in which main temples supported their affiliated temples.
In the Edo period, the [[Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape.== See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also ==" to " == See Also! =="
revision.text:
'''Honmatsu System''' (本末制度) was an administrative system established by the [[Edo period|Edo]] [[bakufu]] (shogunate) to regulate and control Buddhist organizations.
== Background == The ''honmatsu'' system organized Buddhist temples into a hierarchical structure, where ''honji'' (main temples) or ''honzan'' (head temples) held authority over ''matsuji'' (subordinate temples). While this system already existed during the [[Heian period]], it initially functioned as a protective relationship in which main temples supported their affiliated temples.
In the Edo period, the [[Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also! == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.

revision.text:
'''Honmatsu System''' (本末制度) was an administrative system established by the [[Edo period|Edo]] [[bakufu]] (shogunate) to regulate and control Buddhist organizations.
== Background == The ''honmatsu'' system organized Buddhist temples into a hierarchical structure, where ''honji'' (main temples) or ''honzan'' (head temples) held authority over ''matsuji'' (subordinate temples). While this system already existed during the [[Heian period]], it initially functioned as a protective relationship in which main temples supported their affiliated temples.
In the Edo period, the [[Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.

'''Honmatsu System''' (本末制度) was an administrative system established by the [[Edo period|Edo]] [[bakufu]] (shogunate) to regulate and control Buddhist organizations.
== Background == The ''honmatsu'' system organized Buddhist temples into a hierarchical structure, where ''honji'' (main temples) or ''honzan'' (head temples) held authority over ''matsuji'' (subordinate temples). While this system already existed during the [[Heian period]], it initially functioned as a protective relationship in which main temples supported their affiliated temples.
In the Edo period, the [[Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.

'''Honmatsu System''' (本末制度) was an administrative system established by the [[Edo period|Edo]] [[bakufu]] (shogunate) to regulate and control Buddhist organizations.
== Background == The ''honmatsu'' system organized Buddhist temples into a hierarchical structure, where ''honji'' (main temples) or ''honzan'' (head temples) held authority over ''matsuji'' (subordinate temples). While this system already existed during the [[Heian period]], it initially functioned as a protective relationship in which main temples supported their affiliated temples.

In [[Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.

revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
'''Honmatsu System''' (本末制度) was an administrative system established by the [[Edo period|Edo]] [[bakufu]] (shogunate) to regulate and control Buddhist organizations.
== Background == The ''honmatsu'' system organized Buddhist temples into a hierarchical structure, where ''honji'' (main temples) or ''honzan'' (head temples) held authority over ''matsuji'' (subordinate temples). While this system already existed during the [[Heian period]], it initially functioned as a protective relationship in which main temples supported their affiliated temples.
In the Edo period, the [[Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

In the Edo period, the [[Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.

To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.

revision.comment: 16 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:本末制度 up to rev 100852940
revision.text:
'''本末制度'''（ほんまつせいど）は、[[江戸時代]]、[[江戸幕府]]が[[仏教]]教団を統制するために設けた制度である。 ==経緯== 本寺末寺の制度は、本寺とか本山とよばれる大寺が、小寺を末寺とよんでその指揮下におき、統制した制度である。
本寺末寺の関係は、[[平安時代]]にすでに成立しているが、当初は本寺が末寺を保護するという形から成立したものである。[[江戸時代]]に入って、[[江戸幕府]]は寺院をその統制下におくために、
[[寛永]]9年（[[1632年]]）、各宗派の本山に対して「本末帳」の作成を命じ、末寺を調査させた。
その結果、本寺の無い寺が当時かなりあることがわかったので、このような寺院に対して、改めて本末関係を結ばせるよう命じた。
そして[[元禄]]5年（[[1692年]]）に「本末帳」の改訂をおこなわせて、本山から末寺に至る系列を明確にした。
これより先、幕府は[[慶長]]6年（[[1601年]]）の「高野山法度」の制定を皮切りに、
慶長13年（[[1608年]]）から[[元和 (日本)|元和]]2年（[[1616年]]）にかけて各宗各山ごとに法度を制定しており、「本末帳」の作成と併せて、末寺の力を本寺に吸収し、全国の寺院を統制することを図った。
本寺は僧侶の任命・僧階の付与・住職の任免・色衣の着用許可・上人号の執奏などをおこなう権限をもち、本山行事への出仕を命令し、本山費の徴収をおこなうなど、末寺に対して統制をおこない、末寺は本寺に絶対服従を強いられた。
[[寛永]]8年([[1631年]])、新寺の創建を禁止し、翌年以降、各本山に対して「'''末寺帳'''」の提出を義務づけた。これによって、各地方の古刹が幕府の命によって、形式的に特定の宗派に編入されることとなった。
幕府は、[[江戸]]に設置された各宗派の「[[触頭]]（ふれがしら）」を通じて、自らの意向を宗派の末寺に対して周知徹底させることが可能になった。
==関連項目== *[[単立寺院]] ==参考文献== 『品川区史 通史編 上巻』 四 近世編 （六） 生活と文化 1 社寺と信仰 （1）寺院の動き 本末制度 p917 ～p926 東京都品川区 1973年
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ほんまつせいと}} [[Category:江戸時代の政治]] [[Category:江戸時代の仏教]] [[Category:日本の宗教政策]]

== Key Developments == - In [[Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical Ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.

'''Honmatsu System''' (本末制度) was an administrative system established by the [[Edo period|Edo]] [[bakufu]] (shogunate) to regulate and control Buddhist organizations.
== Background == The ''honmatsu'' system organized Buddhist temples into a hierarchical structure, where ''honji'' (main temples) or ''honzan'' (head temples) held authority over ''matsuji'' (subordinate temples). While this system already existed during the [[Heian period]], it initially functioned as a protective relationship in which main temples supported their affiliated temples.
In the Edo period, the [[Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical Ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also == * [[Independent temple]]

To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Honmatsu System''' (本末制度) was an administrative system established by the [[Edo period|Edo]] [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] (shogunate) to regulate and control Buddhist organizations.
== Background == The ''honmatsu'' system organized Buddhist temples into a hierarchical structure, where ''honji'' (main temples) or ''honzan'' (head temples) held authority over ''matsuji'' (subordinate temples). While this system already existed during the [[Heian period]], it initially functioned as a protective relationship in which main temples supported their affiliated temples.
In the Edo period, the [[Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical Ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.

In the Edo period, the [[:en:Edo shogunate|Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[:en:Genna|Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical Ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.

revision.text:
'''Honmatsu System''' (本末制度) was an administrative system established by the [[:en:Edo period|Edo]] [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] (shogunate) to regulate and control Buddhist organizations.
== Background == The ''honmatsu'' system organized Buddhist temples into a hierarchical structure, where ''honji'' (main temples) or ''honzan'' (head temples) held authority over ''matsuji'' (subordinate temples). While this system already existed during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it initially functioned as a protective relationship in which main temples supported their affiliated temples.
In the Edo period, the [[:en:Edo shogunate|Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[:en:Genna|Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical Ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[:en:Genna|Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical Ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:本末制度]][[zh:本末制度]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11520412]] Wikidata name: honmatsu system [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Honmatsu System''' (本末制度) was an administrative system established by the [[:en:Edo period|Edo]] [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] (shogunate) to regulate and control Buddhist organizations.
== Background == The ''honmatsu'' system organized Buddhist temples into a hierarchical structure, where ''honji'' (main temples) or ''honzan'' (head temples) held authority over ''matsuji'' (subordinate temples). While this system already existed during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it initially functioned as a protective relationship in which main temples supported their affiliated temples.
In the Edo period, the [[:en:Edo shogunate|Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[:en:Genna|Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical Ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:本末制度]][[zh:本末制度]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11520412]] Wikidata name: honmatsu system [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Honmatsu System''' (本末制度) was an administrative system established by the [[:en:Edo period|Edo]] [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] (shogunate) to regulate and control Buddhist organizations.
== Background == The ''honmatsu'' system organized Buddhist temples into a hierarchical structure, where ''honji'' (main temples) or ''honzan'' (head temples) held authority over ''matsuji'' (subordinate temples). While this system already existed during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it initially functioned as a protective relationship in which main temples supported their affiliated temples.
In the Edo period, the [[:en:Edo shogunate|Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[:en:Genna|Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical Ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|本末制度|version=100852940|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Buddhism in the Edo period]]
}}

== Background == The ''honmatsu'' system organized Buddhist temples into a hierarchical structure, where ''honji'' (main temples) or ''honzan'' (head temples) held authority over ''matsuji'' (subordinate temples). While this system already existed during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it initially functioned as a protective relationship in which main temples supported their affiliated temples.
In the Edo period, the [[:en:Edo shogunate|Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[:en:Genna|Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''

Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:本末制度]][[zh:本末制度]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:本末制度]][[zh:本末制度]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11520412]] Wikidata name: honmatsu system [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Honmatsu System''' (本末制度) was an administrative system established by the [[:en:Edo period|Edo]] [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] (shogunate) to regulate and control Buddhist organizations.
== Background == The ''honmatsu'' system organized Buddhist temples into a hierarchical structure, where ''honji'' (main temples) or ''honzan'' (head temples) held authority over ''matsuji'' (subordinate temples). While this system already existed during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it initially functioned as a protective relationship in which main temples supported their affiliated temples.
In the Edo period, the [[:en:Edo shogunate|Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[:en:Genna|Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical Ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

While this system already existed during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it initially functioned as a protective relationship in which main temples supported their affiliated temples.
In the Edo period, the [[:en:Edo shogunate|Edo shogunate]] institutionalized this system to exert direct control over Buddhist temples. In [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 9 (1632), the shogunate ordered the creation of ''Honmatsu Registers'' (本末帳) by the head temples of each sect to document their subordinate temples. This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 13 (1608) and [[:en:Genna|Genna]] 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical Ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

This survey revealed many independent temples without formal affiliations, prompting the government to mandate the establishment of hierarchical relationships between such temples and established main temples.
By {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku}} 5 (1692), the ''Honmatsu Registers'' were revised to clarify the chains of command from main temples to subordinate temples.
== Key Developments == - In {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō}} 6 (1601), the "Kōyasan Regulations" were enacted, initiating the shogunate's formal policies for temple governance. - Between {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō}} 13 (1608) and {{ill|Genna|en|Genna|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna}} 2 (1616), sect-specific regulations were established. - In {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei}} 8 (1631), the establishment of new temples was prohibited, and the following year, head temples were required to submit detailed ''Honmatsu Registers.''
The ''honji'' wielded significant authority over ''matsuji,'' including: - Appointing monks and granting clerical Ranks. - Approving the succession of abbots. - Issuing permissions for specific religious practices and attire. - Collecting fees and overseeing participation in temple rituals and events.
Subordinate temples were compelled to comply with their head temple's commands and maintain absolute allegiance.
To centralize its control further, the shogunate established a ''furegashira'' (触頭) system, appointing representatives in Edo to relay the government’s directives to the temples of each sect.
== Impact == The system enforced religious hierarchy, aligning temples with the shogunate’s broader governance objectives. Additionally, ancient temples across regions were formally integrated into specific sects, often under compulsion, reshaping Japan's religious landscape. == See Also == * [[Independent temple]]
== References == - ''Shinagawa City History: General History Edition, Upper Volume,'' Chapter Four: Edo Period, Section Six: Life and Culture, Subsection One: Temples and Beliefs, "(1) The Honmatsu System," pp. 917–926. Shinagawa City, 1973.
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
